metadata.title: Hidden, off-book and contingent sovereign debt - China's undisclosed lending, infrastructure liens as coercive leverage, and the US/global off-balance-sheet stack
metadata.frame: Headline national-debt figures understate true sovereign exposure because states hide liabilities off the books: China lends through SOEs/SPVs and confidential, collateralized contracts that never reach the World Bank's reporting system; the PBoC rolls over distressed borrowers via opaque swap lines; debt converts into control of critical infrastructure (ports, power grids, rail) that forces leaders' hands; and the US (and Japan/EU/UK) carry vast CONTINGENT guarantees - GSE/FHLB/deposit-insurance/entitlement obligations - off the headline number that become real in a crisis. This is the sovereign-scale version of the off-balance-sheet defect mapped in [[macro-uncovered-risk-pools]] and the self-marked-value thread. Real figures where public; debt-trap framing graded contested where scholars dispute it.
metadata.relevance: Adds the sovereign/state layer to the project's hidden-liability theme: complements the private-sector uncovered pools ([[macro-uncovered-risk-pools]]) with state off-book debt; supplies the coercion mechanism (infrastructure liens) behind several geopolitical threads ([[geopolitics-contested-resource-states]], [[geopolitics-cables-space-layer]]); shows the US uncovered pools (GSE/FHLB) ARE implicit sovereign backstops.
metadata.sources: AidData/William & Mary (hidden debt; How China Lends; Why Hide); NBER/AidData Horn-Parks-Reinhart-Trebesch (China LOLR swap lending); BU GDP Center (PBoC swaps); Georgetown JIA + Lowy + CRS + Rhodium + MEI (infrastructure-lien cases); IMF 2025 Art IV China (LGFV); US Treasury FY2024 Financial Report of the US Government (FRUSG); JEC/CRS/GAO/CBO; FDIC/PBGC/HUD/FHLB-OF; Fed/NY Fed (deferred asset, swaps, SRF, ESF); Bundesbank (TARGET2); European Commission (EU-Bonds); Wikipedia (Japan debt); Pinsent Masons (UK PFI). Full URLs in the rendered .md.
metadata.note: AidData's $385B / ~50%-missing figures rest on data through 2017 (the authoritative scholarly baseline, not a current stock); current scope is >$1.1T outstanding. 'Debt-trap diplomacy' is empirically contested for the marquee cases.

china_hidden_lending.summary: China is the world's largest official creditor, and roughly half of its lending to developing countries is undisclosed - intermediated through SOEs/SPVs that escape the World Bank reporting system.

points.detail: AidData (2021): ~$385B of under-reported/undisclosed obligations to China across low-and-middle-income countries (from 13,427 projects worth $843B, 2000-2017); 42 countries carry China debt exposure >10% of GDP. Borrowers under-report obligations by ~5.8% of GDP on average.

China manages >$1.1T in outstanding debt across 20,985 projects in 165 countries (commitments >$1.3T, 2000-2021); 55% of LMIC loans have entered principal repayment, rising to 75% by 2030 (AidData 'Belt and Road Reboot', 2023).

Global debt to China is underestimated by as much as ~50% - about half of loans (2000-2017) were missing from the World Bank/IMF Debtor Reporting System because the borrower is often an SOE/JV/SPV or state bank, not the central government (Horn/Reinhart/Trebesch; AidData 'Why Hide?').

collateralized_contracts.summary: Confidential, collateralized contract terms are the hiding mechanism - and the lien.

points.detail: Across 100 contracts: confidentiality clauses bar borrowers from revealing the terms or even the existence of the debt (universal since 2014); expansive cross-default/cross-cancellation; 'no Paris Club' clauses barring equal-footing restructuring; lender-controlled revenue/escrow accounts as collateral (AidData/CGD/PIIE/Kiel, 2021).

Angola: a $15B oil-prepayment facility collateralized by an offtake assignment + a ~$1.5B-minimum Chinese-held escrow (tapped during a 2020-2023 payment reprieve). Ecuador: ~90% of 2013 oil exports placed under Chinese-firm control. Venezuela: oil proceeds routed through a CDB-domiciled collection account. DRC Sicomines: copper/cobalt-backed infrastructure loan. Resource-backed loans carry ~1ppt higher interest.

pboc_swap_rescue.summary: The PBoC is now an off-book lender of last resort, rolling over distressed BRI borrowers via opaque RMB swap lines outside IMF/World Bank disclosure.

points.detail: ~$240B in rescue/bailout lending across 128 operations to 22 distressed BRI debtors (2000-2021): ~$170B via the PBoC swap-line network + ~$70B from state banks/SOEs; ~80% issued 2016-2021. Swaps fall OUTSIDE international debt-disclosure rules despite multi-year rollovers (Horn/Parks/Reinhart/Trebesch, NBER WP 31105, 2023).

Argentina drew the PBoC swap in 2023 to pay bondholders and the IMF directly in RMB (~$1.7B for an IMF payment) and cleared a $6.5B tranche to defend the peso; PBoC swap draws have at times exceeded half a country's gross reserves (Argentina, Mongolia). Pakistan leaned on the swap to repay maturing foreign debt; ~22% of Pakistan's ~$131B external debt is owed to China (BU GDP Center, 2025).

infrastructure_liens_coercion.summary: Debt converts into control of critical infrastructure - the mechanism that forces leaders' hands. (The blunt 'debt-trap seizure' narrative is contested for the marquee cases; the leverage is real but operates via escrow control, leases, and confidentiality more than outright confiscation.)

points.detail: A 99-year lease of Hambantota port to China Merchants Port (~$1.1B, 2017) is the canonical 'debt-trap seizure' cite - but scholarly consensus (Brautigam; Georgetown) disputes it: Sri Lanka solicited the project and negotiated the lease to raise FX in a balance-of-payments crisis; the lease was separate from the construction loans; China was a fraction of total sovereign debt.

China Southern Power Grid took a 90% stake in the national transmission operator (EDL-T) via a 25-year lease (~$625M), operations commencing Jan 2024 - a tangible transfer of control over critical infrastructure amid debt distress (Laos public-and-publicly-guaranteed debt incl. swaps ~$15.9B in 2023, ~125% of GDP). The China-Laos railway cost ~$5.9B (~1/3 of 2019 GDP).

Djibouti: >half of ~$2.6B external debt to China (ports/rail/pipeline); suspended servicing and secured a 4-year moratorium (2023). Zambia: a $6.3B restructuring incl. $4.1B to China (2023), no seizure. Montenegro: a ~EUR1B Exim Bank highway loan >1/3 of the budget, refinanced/hedged - leverage limited (contested). Pakistan CPEC: ~$26.5B investment projected to cost ~$40B over 20 years, energy IPPs with take-or-pay 'capacity' guarantees; Gwadar port a strategic foothold, not a default-seizure (contested).

lgfv_inside_china.summary: China hides debt at home too - off-budget local-government financing vehicles.

points.detail: IMF 2025 Article IV (Feb 2026): LGFV-channelled project funding ~4% of GDP in 2025; the broad/'augmented' public-sector deficit ~13% of GDP (>14% projected next year); augmented public debt incl. LGFVs pushed toward ~120-150% of GDP. The often-cited ~$7-9T LGFV stock (~50-60% of GDP) is indicative/secondary; the IMF frames it via the augmented ratios.
points.grade: fact (IMF augmented ratios) + weak (the $7-9T stock figure)

us_off_book.summary: The US headline debt is the smallest honest measure - its own accrual accounts and contingent guarantees are multiples larger.

points.detail: Gross national debt ~$38.4T (Dec 2025; ~$30.1T public + ~$7.3T intragovernmental). But the Treasury's own FY2024 Financial Report shows total liabilities $45.5T vs assets $5.7T = a -$39.9T net position - including $15.0T of federal employee/veteran benefits payable that is off the headline.

FRUSG: the 75-year present-value social-insurance shortfall (Social Security + Medicare) is ~$78.3T; the total 75-year fiscal gap (all non-interest spending vs receipts) ~$79.6T, requiring ~4.3% of GDP/yr to close. Treasury labels current policy 'unsustainable.' (The circulated '$175T+' figure is an infinite-horizon advocacy aggregation, not a single FRUSG line - contested.)

GSE guaranteed MBS ~$7.7T (Fannie+Freddie, conservatorship PSPA backstop); FHLB consolidated obligations ~$1.2T; federal student loans ~$1.7T; FHA insurance-in-force ~$1.6T; PBGC (insuring ~30M participants); FDIC deposit insurance backing >$10T with a $153.9B fund (~1.4% coverage); the Fed's $243B deferred asset (cumulative remittance shortfall - avoids reporting negative equity). Each is off the $38T headline; the GSE book alone (2008) and the 2020/2023 facilities show how fast contingent becomes on-book.

The Exchange Stabilization Fund (~$222B, Treasury-controlled, minimal oversight - used for the 2020 backstops); Fed central-bank swap lines (uncapped in crises, ~$580B peak 2020); and the Standing Repo Facility, whose $500B daily cap the FOMC ELIMINATED in Dec 2025 - now an effectively unlimited domestic repo backstop.

other_economies.summary: Same pattern abroad: the headline number sits beside large off-book exposure.

points.detail: Japan: gross debt ~235% of GDP (net ~140%), with the BoJ holding roughly half of JGBs - monetization concentrating rate risk on the central bank. EU: intra-Eurosystem TARGET2 imbalances ~EUR1.06T (Bundesbank claims, 2025) plus ~EUR739B of mutualized EU-Bonds/NextGenEU debt off national books. UK: PFI lifetime repayments will top ~GBP300B on ~GBP55B of capital - the classic off-balance-sheet legacy.

synthesis.summary: Headline debt is a floor; the real exposure is hidden in intermediaries, contingent guarantees, and infrastructure liens - and it coerces.

points.detail: Across China, the US, and other majors, the same defect recurs at the sovereign scale that [[macro-uncovered-risk-pools]] maps in the private sector: liabilities held off the reported number until a forcing event prices them. China hides via SOE/SPV intermediation, confidentiality, escrow liens, and swap-line rollovers; the US via accrual-vs-cash framing, contingent guarantees (the GSE/FHLB pools are literally implicit sovereign backstops), and uncapped facilities.

Indirect debt against critical infrastructure - escrow accounts on oil revenue, 90% leases of a national power grid, take-or-pay capacity guarantees, port concessions - converts a balance-sheet liability into operational control that 'forces the hands of leadership who feel they have no choice.' The strongest cases are escrow/lease control (Angola, Laos), not the contested 'seizure' narrative; the leverage is the confidentiality + collateral + no-restructuring lock, which removes a sovereign's options before any default.

non_claims: No blanket 'debt-trap diplomacy' claim - documented asset seizures on default are rare; the marquee cases (Hambantota, Montenegro, Gwadar) are graded contested, with leverage operating via escrow/lease/confidentiality rather than confiscation.
AidData's $385B / ~50%-missing figures are the scholarly baseline through 2017, NOT a current outstanding stock (current scope >$1.1T).
The US '$175T+' total is an infinite-horizon advocacy aggregation, not an official FRUSG line; the official 75-year gap is ~$78-80T.
China's ~$7-9T LGFV stock is indicative/secondary; the IMF's primary framing is the augmented deficit/debt ratios.
Contingent guarantees are not current cash losses - the point is they are off the headline and become real in a crisis.
Overlay edges are EXCLUDED from the SCC / Z3 / TLA+ proofs.

edges.raw_instrument: collateralized/resource-backed lending with escrow liens + confidentiality/cross-default/no-Paris-Club terms; converts to operational control on distress (Laos national grid 90% lease, Angola oil escrow, Hambantota 99-yr lease) - ~$385B hidden, ~50% missing from World Bank DRS
edges.note: OVERLAY: infrastructure-lien coercion; debt-trap framing contested; excluded from proofs

edges.raw_instrument: ~$240B off-book rescue lending (~$170B RMB swap lines) as lender of last resort, rolled over for years outside IMF/World Bank disclosure; props FX reserves (Argentina, Pakistan)
edges.note: OVERLAY: opaque rollover lending; excluded from proofs

edges.raw_instrument: ~$7-9T off-budget local-government financing vehicles (augmented deficit ~13-14% of GDP, IMF 2025) - hidden inside China's own books
edges.note: OVERLAY: domestic off-budget debt; excluded from proofs

edges.raw_instrument: off the ~$38T headline: GSE MBS ~$7.7T + FHLB ~$1.2T + FDIC DIF $153.9B vs >$10T insured + Fed deferred asset $243B + ~$78-80T 75-yr social-insurance gap (FRUSG total liabilities $45.5T / net -$39.9T)
edges.note: OVERLAY: contingent/accrual exposure off the headline; excluded from proofs

edges.raw_instrument: contingent in normal times, on-book in a crisis - 2008 GSE conservatorship, 2020 ESF/Fed facilities, 2023 SVB rescue each converted off-book exposure into Treasury/Fed cost
edges.note: OVERLAY: crisis conversion channel; excluded from proofs

limits: Maps off-book/hidden/contingent sovereign debt: China's undisclosed lending (~$385B hidden through 2017, >$1.1T outstanding, ~50% missing from World Bank DRS via SOE/SPV intermediation); confidential collateralized contracts (How China Lends - confidentiality/cross-default/no-Paris-Club; Angola/Ecuador/Venezuela/DRC escrow liens); PBoC swap-line rescue lending (~$240B, ~$170B swaps, off-disclosure); infrastructure-lien coercion (Laos grid 90%, Hambantota/CPEC contested); China LGFV (~$7-9T, augmented deficit ~13-14% GDP); US off-book (FRUSG $45.5T liabilities / -$39.9T net; ~$78-80T 75-yr gap; GSE ~$7.7T, FHLB ~$1.2T, FDIC DIF $153.9B vs >$10T, Fed deferred asset $243B, ESF ~$222B, uncapped SRF); Japan/EU/UK (BoJ ~half of JGBs; TARGET2 ~EUR1.06T + EU-Bonds ~EUR739B; UK PFI ~GBP300B). Debt-trap framing graded contested; overlay edges excluded from proofs.

checks.result: ~$385B hidden (AidData 2021, through 2017); >$1.1T outstanding (2023); ~50% missing from World Bank DRS (Horn/Reinhart/Trebesch).
How China Lends: confidentiality/cross-default/no-Paris-Club, escrow collateral; PBoC LOLR ~$240B (~$170B swaps), NBER WP 31105.
Laos CSG 90% grid lease (Jan 2024); Hambantota 99-yr lease (framing contested, Georgetown/Brautigam); Djibouti moratorium; CPEC take-or-pay.
IMF augmented deficit ~13-14% GDP; FRUSG $45.5T liabilities/-$39.9T net, ~$78-80T 75-yr gap; GSE ~$7.7T; Fed deferred asset $243B; TARGET2 ~EUR1.06T; UK PFI ~GBP300B.
